project_summary: Professional upgrade of the current beta zombie rescue shooter into an embeddable web minigame that also runs locally for QA. Product analysis: North Star KPI = 90% of first-time players start an active run within 30 seconds of load. JTBD = give busy users a short, low-friction break with immediately understandable zombie rescue gameplay. Guardrail metric = first playable state appears within 3 seconds on a standard desktop broadband/browser setup after assets are cached for the first session.

product_analysis.north_star_kpi: 90% of first-time players reach an active run within 30 seconds of initial load.
product_analysis.jtbd: Give busy users a short, low-friction break with an immediately understandable rescue shooter session they can play inside another web surface.
product_analysis.guardrail_metric: First playable state appears within 3 seconds on a standard modern desktop browser after the initial asset load for a repeat session.

user_personas.goal: Start a short session immediately and understand who to protect and who to shoot without reading a manual.
user_personas.pain_point: Low tolerance for setup friction, unclear rules, and delayed feedback.

user_personas.goal: Embed the game inside a bounded web container and trust that it behaves consistently without extra user setup.
user_personas.pain_point: Embedded experiences often break layout constraints, load too slowly, or feel unfinished.

user_personas.goal: Validate that the game is coherent, runnable locally, and aligned with the intended product direction.
user_personas.pain_point: Legacy docs, code, and tests can drift and make it hard to judge readiness.

no_go_zone: No backend server, user accounts, or cloud persistence — this delivery remains a self-contained web game with local-only persistence.
No multiplayer, matchmaking, or shared-session features — the game is single-player only.
No engine rewrite away from Phaser in this delivery — the current implementation baseline is upgraded rather than replaced.
No runtime dependency on third-party HTTP APIs or remote executable scripts — gameplay must stay self-contained.
No realistic gore or graphic horror presentation — violence stays stylized and office-safe.

functional_requirements.title: Fast-start embeddable session
functional_requirements.description: The game must support a short-session embedded play flow while remaining runnable locally for development and QA, with the first playable state appearing quickly and without extra installation for the end user.
acceptance_criteria: A local operator can reach the first playable state with the documented setup flow in 5 minutes or less from the repository root on a standard Node.js environment.
In a standard 800x450 embedded container, the game renders without clipping the main playfield or hiding primary controls.
A first-time player can start an active run within 30 seconds of the initial load using only visible on-screen instructions.

functional_requirements.title: Readable rescue-shooter core loop
functional_requirements.description: The active play loop must make the player protect civilians moving toward a bunker while shooting zombies through a fixed-camera aiming model with immediate, legible outcomes.
acceptance_criteria: The custom crosshair remains visible throughout active gameplay and primary targets can be distinguished in less than 1 second by a first-time observer.
The player can aim with the mouse and fire with left click in every active gameplay state without additional tutorial steps.
A first-time player can identify the bunker as the civilian destination and safe endpoint within 30 seconds of entering the first run.

functional_requirements.title: Deterministic target and penalty rules
functional_requirements.description: Shots, civilian loss, and enemy contact must follow a single coherent rule set so that mistakes and threats always produce the same observable consequences.
acceptance_criteria: A player-shot civilian immediately applies -15 score and -10 life with negative visual and audio feedback in the same interaction or next rendered frame.
Normal, elite/alpha, and brute zombie contact apply 10, 15, and 20 life damage respectively and each successful contact increments civilians lost by exactly 1.
When multiple entities overlap under the crosshair, hit resolution returns the same target outcome across repeated identical overlap conditions.

functional_requirements.title: Escalating pressure with three zombie categories
functional_requirements.description: The game must increase pressure across levels while preserving three distinct zombie categories and a flexible mixed-threat wave composition that supports civilian protection decisions.
acceptance_criteria: The game exposes exactly three enemy categories in the product baseline: normal zombie, elite/alpha zombie, and brute zombie.
Each level increases threat through at least one measurable factor such as spawn rate, active threat volume, enemy durability, or chase pressure.
Wave composition includes both threat and protection pressure, but requirements do not depend on a fixed zombie-to-civilian spawn ratio.

functional_requirements.title: Scoring, level closure, and record persistence
functional_requirements.description: The game must track score, level state, and end-of-run outcome clearly, and retain the highest score locally so players can compare results across sessions.
acceptance_criteria: The HUD displays current score, level, civilians saved, civilians lost, and current or best run context during active gameplay.
A run ends with a clear game-over or level-closure state that reports the player's achieved score before restart or continuation.
The highest recorded score persists across a browser reload in 100% of standard local runs where localStorage is available.

functional_requirements.title: Professional presentation quality baseline
functional_requirements.description: The product must ship with final-quality visual, UI, and audio feedback that reads as a finished casual minigame rather than a technical prototype.
acceptance_criteria: Gameplay-critical characters, bunker, crosshair, and HUD use a consistent visual style with no placeholder geometry or mixed final-art styles in the shipped build.

Every major action class — shooting, zombie hit, zombie death, civilian damage, level complete, and game over — has a distinct feedback signal, with audio playback starting within 100ms of the trigger event on an unlocked audio context.
HUD text remains legible at 800x450 and 1280x720 with no overlap that obscures score, level, civilians saved, civilians lost, or accuracy.
functional_requirements.implementation_notes: Audio feedback is implemented via src/audio/AudioManager.js (procedural Web Audio synthesis, SFX dispatch, ambient loop). UI string constants (overlay copy, HUD labels, button text) are centralized in src/locale/ui.js and consumed by UIScene.

functional_requirements.title: Local asset provenance and runtime safety
functional_requirements.description: All shipped gameplay-critical assets and runtime behaviors must be traceable, local, and safe for a browser-embedded environment.
acceptance_criteria: 100% of gameplay-critical visual and audio assets loaded by the shipped runtime are listed in the project asset inventory and documented in ASSETS.md.
The browser runtime performs no gameplay-critical remote script execution and no third-party executable asset loading during a standard session.
The embedded or local runtime exposes no admin or debug route to an end user during normal play.
functional_requirements.implementation_notes: Asset provenance validation is handled by src/modules/module-zombite3-service/assetsPolicy.js and securityPolicy.js. Difficulty scaling and wave pacing logic is implemented as the pure function getDifficultyProfile() in src/modules/module-zombite3-service/gameRules.js, extracted from GameScene to support independent testing.

user_stories.i_want: to start a run immediately inside a web page or local test build
user_stories.so_that: I can play without setup friction

user_stories.i_want: to understand targets, threats, and safe goals immediately
user_stories.so_that: the game feels readable and fair

user_stories.i_want: friendly fire and zombie contact to follow one consistent rule set
difficulty to escalate through distinct zombie categories and rising pressure
user_stories.so_that: the session remains tense and replayable

user_stories.i_want: my run results and high score to be visible and persistent
user_stories.so_that: I can compare performance across sessions

user_stories.i_want: the game to look and sound finished
user_stories.so_that: it feels like a real product and not a prototype

user_stories.i_want: runtime assets and browser behavior to be local and traceable
user_stories.so_that: the game is safe to embed and maintain

non_functional_requirements.requirement: The game must reach a playable embedded or local session quickly enough for short-break usage.
non_functional_requirements.acceptance_criteria: On a standard modern desktop browser, the first playable state appears within 3 seconds for a repeat session after the initial asset load.

non_functional_requirements.requirement: The game must remain understandable to first-time players with minimal instruction.
non_functional_requirements.acceptance_criteria: At least 90% of first-time internal test players can identify the goal, valid targets, and loss condition within 30 seconds of first playable state.

non_functional_requirements.requirement: The repository must support repeatable development verification from the root project workspace.
non_functional_requirements.acceptance_criteria: Documented local setup, automated tests, and production build commands complete successfully from the repository root.

non_functional_requirements.requirement: Gameplay-critical runtime behavior must remain local, deterministic, and safe for embedding.
non_functional_requirements.acceptance_criteria: A standard session performs no runtime fetch of executable third-party scripts and no unbounded external control surface is exposed to end users.

constraints: Continue from the current Phaser 3 + Vite implementation baseline.
Support local execution for development and QA while targeting embeddable web delivery.
No backend, multiplayer, online accounts, or cloud save.
Assets must remain local and documented in ASSETS.md.
technology_preferences: Desktop-first browser experience with embeddable container support
